title: Pulling and attaching a clay handle
subtitle: Drawing a strap handle from a clay lump and fixing it to a leather-hard mug or jug
excerpt: A pulled handle is drawn from a lump of clay by repeatedly stroking it downward with a wet hand to form a strap. The handle is then bent to shape, scored and slipped at both attachment points, and pressed firmly onto the pot. Pulling produces a stronger handle than extruding because the drawing motion aligns the clay particles along the handle length.
sourceNotes: Handle pulling and attachment technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
pulled handle

requires no tools beyond a wet hand and a lump of clay. The pulling action aligns the clay particles along the handle length, making the finished handle stronger than one cut from a slab. The pot must be at
leather-hard
before attachment; soft clay will distort, bone-dry clay will not bond. The handle is pulled first, bent to the curve of the pot, then attached using
score and slip
Pulling the handle
1. Start with a thick clay lump
Take about 80 g of well-wedged clay and form it into a thick cone shape, roughly 80 mm tall and 40 mm wide at the base. Hold the thick end of the cone in one hand with the point facing downward.
2. Wet the other hand and draw downward

Wet the free hand with water. Wrap the thumb and index finger around the clay below the holding hand and draw downward with a firm, even pressure; the clay elongates. Rotate the clay cone slightly between each stroke so the thinning is even. Repeat the drawing motion eight to twelve times, re-wetting the hand as needed, until the clay forms a strap roughly 150-180 mm long and 20 mm wide.
3. Shape and curve the handle

Hold the strap over the pot and bend it into the curve of the handle: a gentle arc. Lay the curved handle on a board. Let it firm for 10-20 minutes until it holds the curve without collapsing. The handle should not be fully leather-hard at attachment; slightly softer than the pot is ideal so it bonds fully.
Attaching the handle
4. Mark the attachment positions

Hold the handle against the pot and mark the top and bottom attachment points with a light scratch. The top attachment is typically just below the rim; the bottom attachment is two-thirds of the way down the body.
5. Score and slip both surfaces

At both attachment points on the pot and at both ends of the handle, score a crosshatch pattern with the needle tool. Apply liquid slip to all four scored areas. Press the top of the handle onto the upper attachment point, blending the clay from the handle end into the pot wall with a fingertip. Curve the handle to the marked lower position and press and blend the lower end.
What it looks like when it is right

The handle curves cleanly away from the pot with a thumb-width gap at the widest point. Both ends blend smoothly into the pot wall with no visible join line. The handle should feel firm and not wobble when pressed lightly. After drying, the join is as solid as the surrounding clay; no cracks visible at the attachment points.

items.fix: Score thoroughly and apply slip to both surfaces; match the dryness; the handle should be slightly softer than the pot
items.cause: Scoring or slipping was incomplete, or the handle and pot were at very different drying stages
items.symptom: Handle cracks at the join after drying

items.fix: Let the pulled handle firm for 15-20 minutes before attaching; prop the handle temporarily with a small wad of soft clay if needed
items.cause: Handle attached when still too soft

items.fix: Keep the hand wet throughout; draw slowly and evenly; the clay elongates gradually, not in one pull
items.cause: Hand too dry, or pulling too fast
